1: 1 Samuel narrates the transition from the period of the judges to the monarchy (ca. 1100-1011 BCE). Its theological structure revolves around three figures: Samuel (the last and greatest judge, the transitional prophet-priest-judge), Saul (the failed king, rejected because of disobedience), and David (the man after God's own heart, the model for the Davidic covenant). The contrast between Saul and David is theologically instructive: Saul has the outward appearance (tall, handsome, from the right tribe) but lacks the inner heart-alignment; David lacks the outward qualifications (youngest, overlooked, a shepherd) but has the heart that YHWH seeks (1 Samuel 16:7: YHWH looks on the heart). This contrast between external appearance and internal reality runs from 1 Samuel through Paul's theology of the Spirit vs. the flesh.
3: Elkanah's annual pilgrimage to Shiloh situates the narrative in the final phase of the tabernacle period. Shiloh (šîlōh, modern Khirbet Seilun, in the hill country of Ephraim) was the central sanctuary from after the conquest (Joshua 18:1, when the tabernacle was erected there) through the end of the judges period, a span of roughly 300 years (ca. 1400-1050 BCE). Archaeological excavations at Khirbet Seilun by Israel Finkelstein (1981-84) revealed substantial Iron Age I occupation consistent with a cultic center, including large quantities of imported pottery and faunal remains suggesting sacrificial feasting. The annual pilgrimage pattern (Elkanah going 'year by year') reflects the Pentateuchal requirements of Exodus 23:14-17 and Deuteronomy 16:16 — the three annual pilgrim festivals (Passover, Weeks, Booths). The mention of YHWH of armies (YHWH ṣᵉḇāʾôt) in v3 and v11 is significant: this is the first occurrence of this specific divine title in the narrative books, and it becomes the dominant epithet in the Ark Narrative that follows in 1 Samuel 4-6.
9: The Shiloh sanctuary has a 'doorpost of the temple of YHWH' (mᵉzûzōt hêḵal YHWH) — the Hebrew hêḵal here refers to the tabernacle's tent structure rather than a permanent building (which is not yet built). The term hêḵal (palace/temple) applied to the tabernacle is unusual; it anticipates the transition that will occur in 2 Sam 7 when David proposes to build a permanent bêt (house/temple) for YHWH. That the Shiloh tabernacle is called a hêḵal suggests either that the original tent had been supplemented with more permanent structures by this period, or that the narrator is using the term proleptically. The Priestly legislation of Num 3-4 describes the Levites dismantling and transporting the tabernacle; the use of hêḵal here suggests a period of relative stability at Shiloh where the tabernacle had taken on more permanent character.
20: Hannah names her son šᵉmûʾēl (Samuel) with the explanation: kî mēYHWH šᵉʾiltîw — 'for from YHWH I asked him.' The etymology offered is from the root šāʾal (to ask/request), making the name mean 'name of God' or 'heard of God' — though the Semitic derivation of šᵉmûʾēl more naturally yields 'his name is El' or 'heard of God' (combining šēm + ʾēl or šāmaʿ + ʾēl). The narrative etymology — connecting the name to Saul's being asked/lent — actually fits Saul's name (šāʾûl, asked for) better than Samuel's. Scholars have noted this as a possible narrative telescoping or theologically motivated name-giving. Whatever the historical-linguistic analysis, the narrative point is clear: Samuel is the answered prayer, the gift-child, the one whose life belongs to YHWH because he was received from YHWH's hand.
2: Elkanah had two wives — Hannah and Peninnah — a polygamous household legally permitted under Mosaic law but never idealized in the narrative. Peninnah had children; Hannah had none. The names are significant: Hannah (channah) = grace/favor; Peninnah = coral/pearl (a name associated with beauty and fecundity). The barren-plus-fertile co-wife pattern echoes Rachel and Leah (Gen 29-30), Sarah and Hagar (Gen 16), and anticipates Elizabeth and the societal pressure of her barrenness (Luke 1:25). In all these cases, the divinely significant birth comes through the woman marked by apparent disadvantage.
4: At the time of his annual sacrifice, Elkanah distributed portions (manot) to Peninnah and all her children. The distribution of sacrificial meat was the shared meal component of the shelamim (peace/fellowship offering), eaten by the worshiper and family rather than consumed on the altar. Deuteronomy 16:14 specifies that festival celebrations should include household members — but the unequal distribution here becomes the social fault line of the narrative. The sacrifice meant to foster family solidarity instead exposes its fracture.
5: Elkanah gave Hannah a special portion “because he loved her, even though the LORD had closed her womb.” The narrator attributes Hannah’s barrenness to YHWH directly: ki-et Hannah ahev vaYHWH sagar rachamah. This is the specific narrative theology of the barren-woman pattern: YHWH closes the womb of the one through whom he will work, so that the birth is unambiguously an act of divine grace. The closed womb is the signature of a special divine design (cf. Genesis 20:18; 1 Samuel 25:21; 1 Samuel 29:31; Judges 13:2-3).
6: Peninnah (tzaratah, her rival/adversary — the word also denotes a co-wife, related to tzarah, trouble/distress) provoked Hannah bitterly to irritate her “because the LORD had closed her womb.” The social pressure of barrenness in the ancient Near East was crushing: childlessness implied divine disfavor and meant no heir. Peninnah weaponizes this cultural shame. The Hebrew ka’as (provoke, vex) denotes deep emotional pain rather than surface annoyance.
7: “This went on year after year” — the annual pilgrimage to Shiloh (the central sanctuary during the judges period, located in Ephraim approximately 30 km north of Jerusalem) becomes the occasion for repeated torment. Shiloh housed the tabernacle ark after the conquest (Joshua 18:1) and functioned as the primary sacrificial center for roughly 300 years until its destruction, likely by the Philistines after the battle of Aphek (1 Sam 4), as confirmed by Jeremiah 7:12: “Go now to my place that was in Shiloh, where I made my name dwell at first, and see what I did to it.” Hannah’s grief interrupts the festival meal: she weeps and cannot eat.
8: Elkanah’s question — “Am I not more to you than ten sons?” — is well-intentioned but tone-deaf. He interprets Hannah’s grief as inadequate appreciation for his love, missing that her sorrow is about her standing before God and society. Ten (asarah) is a symbolic number of completeness. Elkanah’s consolation echoes the human attempts to compensate for what only God can give — paralleled when the disciples try to redirect the blind man’s cry to Jesus (Mark 10:48). Human provision cannot substitute for divine fulfillment.
10: Hannah was “deeply distressed (marat nefesh, bitter of soul)” and prayed to YHWH weeping bitterly (bakoh tivkeh, the infinitive absolute intensifying the main verb). Her approach to YHWH is unmediated, bypassing the priestly intermediary. The juxtaposition with Eli sitting by the doorpost emphasizes that Hannah goes directly to God. This directness — the bitterly distressed woman approaching YHWH herself — is a prototype of the access to God Jesus teaches in Luke 18:1-8 (the persistent widow) and embodies in his own intercessory approach.
11: Hannah’s vow is conditional (neder): if YHWH gives her a son, she will give him back to YHWH all his days, and “no razor shall touch his head (mora lo ya’aleh al rosho).” This is the Nazirite dedication formula from Numbers 6:5. Hannah consecrates her unborn son to a lifetime Nazirite vow — a state of special holiness. The only lifetime Nazirites in the OT are Samson (Judges 13:5) and Samuel himself; John the Baptist shares the pattern (Luke 1:15: “he must never drink wine or other fermented drink”). Hannah’s radical act: she asks for a son in order to give him away permanently. The gift returned to the giver is the defining logic of consecration.
12: Eli, positioned as the professional guardian of sacred space, watches Hannah’s mouth while she prays. His surveillance is that of a professional arbiter of propriety rather than a spiritual companion. Ancient Near Eastern prayer was normally audible — Mesopotamian and Egyptian prayer literature describes spoken petitions, and the temple liturgy assumed vocal confession and petition. Silent interior prayer was rare enough that Eli’s misinterpretation is historically plausible, not merely character deficiency. He sees lip movement without sound and concludes intoxication.
13: Hannah speaks in her heart (medabberet al libbah); only her lips move. This is the first description of purely interior prayer in the Hebrew Bible — prayer as direct address to God from the inner person without performance or audibility. Psalms 19:14 (“may the meditation of my heart be pleasing to you”) and later inward-prayer spirituality builds on this. The contrast between Hannah’s interior intensity and the formal temple setting makes her an emblem of authentic spiritual communication. Jesus’ teaching in Matthew 6:6 — “pray to your Father who is in secret” — elevates the principle of private, non-performative prayer that Hannah models here.
14: Eli’s rebuke reveals the failure of discernment: he mistakes genuine spiritual distress for its social opposite (intoxication). Festivals did involve feasting (Amos 2:8 condemns those who drink wine beside altars), so drunkenness at the sanctuary was not unknown. The misreading of genuine spiritual experience as disorder is a recurrent pattern: the Pentecost outpouring is similarly misread (Acts 2:13: “They are full of new wine”). Eli’s accusation is contextually plausible but spiritually wrong.
15: Hannah’s self-defense: “I have been pouring out my soul (eshtopek et napshi) before the LORD.” The phrase “pouring out my soul” (shaphak nefesh) is a striking image of total self-disclosure to God. Psalms 62:8 uses the same idiom: “Pour out your heart before him.” Lamentations 2:19: “Pour out your heart like water before the face of the Lord.” The distinction between yayin (wine) and shekar (strong drink) encompasses the full range of fermented beverages — Hannah’s denial is total.
16: “Do not take your servant for a worthless woman (bat beliyya’al).” The term beliyya’al (literally “without profit/worth”) is used elsewhere in 1 Samuel for Eli’s own sons (1 Samuel 2:12: benei beliyya’al). Hannah protests that she is not what Eli’s corrupt sons represent. The irony that the priest who cannot identify genuine piety is the father of the most glaring examples of impiety deepens throughout the narrative.
17: Eli’s priestly benediction shifts the encounter from accusation to blessing: “Go in peace (lekhi le-shalom), and may the God of Israel grant your petition (she’elah) that you have asked of him.” The word she’elah (petition) connects to the child’s name: Samuel (Shemu’el), explained etymologically as “asked of God” in 1 Samuel 1:20 — though the etymology may also play on shema’ + el (God has heard). The name Saul (sha’ul) will later haunt this etymology: the king the people “asked for” versus the servant of God who was “asked from” YHWH.
18: After Eli’s blessing, Hannah returned and ate, and “her face was no longer downcast.” The transformation precedes the actual granting of the request — she departs in trust before the pregnancy. This faith-before-fulfillment pattern is characteristic of the prayer-of-faith model: the answer to prayer is received in trust before it is received in fact. Hebrews 11:1 defines faith as “the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.” Hannah demonstrates this: the petition lodged with God has the quality of completed action in the heart before it materializes in history.
19: The next morning they worshiped and returned to Ramah. “The LORD remembered her (vayizkerah YHWH).” Divine remembrance (zakar) is a covenant term throughout the OT: YHWH remembered Noah (Genesis 8:1), remembered Abraham (Genesis 19:29), remembered Rachel (Genesis 30:22). The remembrance is not mere mental recall but active attention that issues in transformative action. The same verb is used for YHWH’s covenant faithfulness in Exodus 2:24 (“God remembered his covenant with Abraham”). God’s memory creates history.
21: Elkanah went up to offer the yearly sacrifice and fulfill his vow. Hannah did not accompany him, explaining she would wait until the child is weaned. Ancient Near Eastern weaning occurred at approximately two to three years of age. Numbers 6:13-21 specifies that the completion of a Nazirite vow required a presentation at the sanctuary with specified offerings; Hannah’s delay until weaning ensures Samuel is old enough to remain at the sanctuary independently.
22: Hannah’s plan: “As soon as the child is weaned, I will bring him, that he may appear before the LORD and remain there permanently (ve-yashav sham ’ad-’olam).” The word ’olam (forever, perpetually) signals the irreversibility of the vow. The child-given-back-permanently prefigures the gift of the Son: “God so loved the world that he gave his only Son” (John 3:16) — the supreme gift that does not return. Hannah’s sacrifice of her son is a type of the Father’s gift of his Son.
23: Elkanah defers to Hannah: “Only, may the LORD establish his word (yaqem YHWH et devaro).” This prayer for the establishment of the divine word anticipates a theological principle central to all prophetic texts: divine words are not mere statements but self-realizing acts that YHWH “establishes” in history. Isaiah 55:11: “so shall my word be that goes out from my mouth; it shall not return to me empty, but it shall accomplish that which I purpose.”
24: At weaning, Hannah brought Samuel to Shiloh with a substantial offering: a three-year-old bull, an ephah of flour (approximately 22 liters), and a skin of wine. The offering exceeds the normal Nazirite completion offering of Numbers 6:14, suggesting a celebratory freewill offering beyond the vow requirements. The note “the child was still young (veha-na’ar na’ar)” uses the term na’ar twice in the same sentence — a rare reduplication that emphasizes the poignancy of bringing such a small child for permanent dedication.
25: They slaughtered the bull and brought the child to Eli. The slaughter precedes the presentation; the offering and the dedicant are brought together in the same gesture. The imagery of sacrifice accompanying the presentation of a consecrated son surfaces again in Luke 2:22-24, when Mary and Joseph bring Jesus to the temple with the required offering (two turtledoves). Luke’s nativity account is constructed with the Samuel narrative as a template: barren mothers, priestly figures, hymns of reversal (Hannah’s Song / Magnificat), and the child dedicated at the sanctuary.
26: Hannah presents herself to Eli with the idiomatic oath formula: “As you live, my lord.” She identifies herself as the woman who prayed at this very spot. The emphasis on location — nitzavti immekha bazeh (standing with you here) — marks the exact site of her prayer as sacred. Hannah returns to the point of her deepest distress to complete the covenant transaction. The same spot where Eli misread her as drunk is where she fulfills her vow. Sacred geography often works this way: the place of encounter becomes the place of fulfillment (Genesis 28:16-22: Jacob’s vow at Bethel completed at Bethel).
27: “For this child I prayed (el-ha-na’ar hazeh hitpallalti), and the LORD has granted me my petition (sha’elati) that I asked of him.” The connection between petition and answer is explicit and personal: this specific child is the answer to this specific prayer. The word sha’elati (my petition) will be embedded in the child’s name. The pattern of prayer-answered-in-a-person is structurally significant: Isaac (Abraham’s intercession, Genesis 20:17), Samson (his mother’s vow, Judg 13), John the Baptist (Elizabeth’s answered prayer, Luke 1:13), and ultimately Jesus as the deepest answer to all Israel’s petitions.
28: “Therefore I have given him back (ve-gam anokhi hish’iltîhu) to the LORD. As long as he lives, he belongs to the LORD.” The verb sha’al (to ask/lend) takes on a technical sense: Hannah “lends” Samuel back to YHWH — another reading of the name: “the one lent to God.” The act of giving back what was asked for is the culmination of the logic of grace: Hannah receives the gift in order to return it. Deuteronomy 26:1-11 ritualizes first fruits on this same logic: you receive the land’s produce as YHWH’s gift, then offer it back in acknowledgment of the source.
